coredata.dc:title: Efficient visible-light-driven photoreduction of U(VI) by carbon dots modified porous g-C3N4
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Zhongran
Zhao, Shang
Lian, Junjie
Li, Le
Ding, Dexin
coredata.dc:description: The efficient removal of uranium from radioactive wastewater is of great significance for the ecological environment safety and sustainable development of nuclear energy. Herein, the carbon dots modified porous graphitic carbon nitride (CNCD) was fabricated and used for photoreduction of U(VI) under visible LED light irradiation. The removal rate of U(VI) by CNCD-2 could reach more than 95 % over a wide range of U(VI) concentration. In addition, the CNCD-2 showed excellent selectivity and recyclability for photoreduction of U(VI). Further mechanism studies showed that the high photocatalysis activity of CNCD-2 was attributed to the effective separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, strong visible light absorption capacity and narrow bandgap. The U(VI) was activated by both photoinduced e- and •O2 – in the process of photoreduction, and then immobilized by transforming it to metastudtite ((UO2)O2·2H2O). These results indicate that the CNCD is a promising material for remediation of uranium-containing wastewater under visible light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: U(VI)
Removal
Adsorption
Photoreduction
Wastewater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Excellent Young Scholars (2020JJ3028); the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (21A0259); and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51704170, 12175103 ). Additionally, we thank Yuan Gao (China University of Mining and Technology) for his helpful discussion.

This work was supported by the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Excellent Young Scholars (2020JJ3028); the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (21A0259); and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51704170, 12175103). Additionally, we thank Yuan Gao (China University of Mining and Technology) for his helpful discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) TEM images of g-C3N4 and CNCD-2, recpectively; (c) HRTEM image of CNCD-2; (d) TEM image and (e-i) TEM elemental mapping of CNCD-2-U.
(a) FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4 and CNCD; (b) XPS spectra of g-C3N4, CDs and CNCD; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (c and e) and N 1 s (d and f) of g-C3N4 and CNCD-2.
(a) XRD patterns of g-C3N4 and CNCD; (b) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms.

(a) Photoreduction and (b) adsorption of U(VI) by g-C3N4 and CNCD (m = 5 mg, V = 30 mL, CU(VI) = 20 mg/L, pH = 5.0 ± 0.02, T = 298 K); (c) Photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD-2 at different pH values (m = 5 mg, V = 30 mL, C U(VI) = 20 mg/L, T = 298 K); (d) Zeta potentials of CNCD-2; (e) Percent of U(VI) species in aqueous solution at different pH values; (f) Photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD-2 at different initial U(VI) concentrations (m = 5 mg, V = 30 mL, pH = 5.0 ± 0.02, T = 298 K).

(a) Photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD-2 in the presence of interfering ions (m = 5 mg, V = 30 mL, Cmetal ions = 20 mg/L, pH = 5.0 ± 0.02, T = 298 K); (b) Cycle tests for photoreduction of U(VI) (m = 5 mg, V = 30 mL, CU(VI) = 20 mg/L, pH = 5.0 ± 0.02, T = 298 K); (c) FT-IR spectra of CNCD-2, U-loaded CNCD-2 and U-desorbed CNCD-2; (d) XRD patterns of CNCD-2 and U-desorbed CNCD-2.

(a) XPS spectra of CNCD-2 and CNCD-2-U; (b) U 4f XPS spectra of CNCD-2-U after adsorption and photoreduction of U(VI); (c) XRD patterns of CNCD-2 and CNCD-2-U; (d) PL spectra, (e) Transient photocurrent responses and (f) EIS Nyquist plots of g-C3N4 and CNCD.
(a) UV–Vis DRS spectra, (b) Kubelka–Munk plot of g-C3N4 and CNCD; Valance band XPS of (c) g-C3N4, (d) CNCD-1, (e) CNCD-2 and (f) CNCD-3.
(a) Energy level of g-C3N4 and CNCD; (b) photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD-2 with various radical scavenger; EPR spectra of (c) DMPO-•OH and (d) DMPO-•O2 − in light or dark.
Diagram showing the possible mechanism of photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD.

head.ce:title: Efficient visible-light-driven photoreduction of U(VI) by carbon dots modified porous g-C3N4

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Discipline Laboratory for National Defense for Biotechnology in Uranium Mining and Hydrometallurgy, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Discipline Laboratory for National Defense for Biotechnology in Uranium Mining and Hydrometallurgy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Discipline Laboratory for National Defense for Biotechnology in Uranium Mining and Hydrometallurgy, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficient removal of uranium from radioactive wastewater is of great significance for the ecological environment safety and sustainable development of nuclear energy. Herein, the carbon dots modified porous graphitic carbon nitride (CNCD) was fabricated and used for photoreduction of U(VI) under visible LED light irradiation. The removal rate of U(VI) by CNCD-2 could reach more than 95 % over a wide range of U(VI) concentration. In addition, the CNCD-2 showed excellent selectivity and recyclability for photoreduction of U(VI). Further mechanism studies showed that the high photocatalysis activity of CNCD-2 was attributed to the effective separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, strong visible light absorption capacity and narrow bandgap. The U(VI) was activated by both photoinduced e- and •O2 – in the process of photoreduction, and then immobilized by transforming it to metastudtite ((UO2)O2·2H2O). These results indicate that the CNCD is a promising material for remediation of uranium-containing wastewater under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of green and clean energy, nuclear energy has attracted extensive attention. Uranium (U) is a main nuclear fuel and is also a kind of heavy metal element that is both radiotoxicity and chemical toxicity [1]. With the development of uranium mining, nuclear fuel cycle and spent fuel disposal, a large number of uranium-containing wastewater will inevitably be produced. Uranium will migrate into the environment and even the food chain with the wastewater, thus posing a potential threat to the ecological environment [2–3]. Thus, it is of great significance to efficiently remove U(IV) from the uranium-containing wastewater.

There are two main types of uranium in the natural environment: soluble hexavalent uranium U(VI) and almost insoluble tetravalent uranium U(IV). At present, many methods have been used to remove U(VI) from uranium-containing wastewater, including ion exchange, precipitation, membrane separation, adsorption, etc. [4–11]. Among them, photocatalysis reduction of U(VI) to U(IV) has attracted wide attention due to its low cost, high efficiency and strong selectivity [12–19]. As a promising material, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been widely used in the photoreduction of U(VI) because of its low cost, chemical stability non-toxicity and visible light activity [20–21]. However, the application of g-C3N4 is remain constrained by the water solubility, low quantum efficiency and large bandwidth [22–23]. In order to overcome these shortcomings, various methods have been proposed to promote the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 for U(VI), including surface modification, atomic doping and construction of heterojunction [24–29]. Ke et al. [30] reported that the photoreduction performance of SC3N4 for uranium was increased significantly after doping S, which was 2.28 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. Li et al. [31] constructed a CdS/g-C3N4 nanocomposites for reducing U(VI), the results showed that the removal rate of U(VI) by CdS/g-C3N4 was 64.1 and 9.7 times higher than that of g-C3N4 and CdS, respectively. Our previous studies also found that the photocatalytic capacity of g-C3N4 for U(VI) can significantly be enhanced by the construction of heterojunction with rational design [32–33].

As a type of carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been proved to facilitate the transport of photo-generated charges as an electron acceptor. Meanwhile, CDs have the advantages of low toxicity, visible light response, low cost and easy preparation [34–36]. Recently, many studies have indicated that the photocatalytic capacity of g-C3N4 could significantly be improved by CDs modification, including the visible-light-driven hydrogen production, organic matter degradation and carbon dioxide reduction [37–39].

Therefore, in this work, the CDs modified porous g-C3N4 (denoted as CNCD) was synthesized and characterized by TEM, FT-IR, BET, XPS and XRD. The photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD was systematically tested under visible LED light irradiation. To investigate the photoreduction mechanism of U(VI), TEM, EPR, EIS, FL, XPS, XRD and UV–Vis DRS were employed to evaluate and analyze the reduction process and products. Meanwhile, the selectivity and recyclability of CNCD for photoreduction of U(VI) were also explored in detail.

The synthetic CDs were referred to in previous literature [40]. Carbon dots modified porous g-C3N4 (CNCD) was synthesized by thermal condensation [41]. Firstly, 45 mL 0.35 M nitric acid was poured into 60 mL ethylene glycol solution with 2 g melamine and kept stirring for 10 min. The mixed solution was filtered to obtain a white solid intermediate. Afterwards, the intermediate was dissolved in 80 mL ethanol and then the appropriate amount of CDs was added by stirring for 1 h. The mixture was filtered and the resulting brown solid was washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the CNCD was obtained by calcining the brown solid at 550 °C (heating rate: 2 °C/min, 4 h).

The porous g-C3N4 was obtained using the above method without adding CDs. When the volume of CDs was 400 μL, 800 μL and 1200 μL, the as-synthesized samples were denoted as CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3, respectively.

The morphology of as-synthesized material was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010 instrument), its chemical compositions were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB 250XI), its structure information was characterized by FT-IR spectra (Prestige-21), its crystal structure was identified by powder X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Ultima IV), its N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2020), its radicals were characterized by electron paramagnetic resonance (Bruker A300), its Zeta potential at pH = 3–7 was characterized on Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS 90, its bandgaps were characterized by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600), its Photoluminescence (PL) was characterized by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS980), and its electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent were characterized by an electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Co. Shanghai China) in 0.5 M Na2SO4 with a three electrode system.

Unless otherwise specified, the photoreduction experiments were takes place in an air bath shaker at 298 K. Typically, photocatalyst (5 mg) and methanol (1 mL) were added to a 30 mL of 20 mg/L uranium solution at pH 5.0. An 8 W LED light (400–700 nm) was used as the visible light source. The pH of solution was adjusted by 0.1 M HCl and/or NaOH solution. After a certain time, 2.0 mL solution was taken out by syringe and filtered by a membrane filter (0.22 μm) for further analysis. The concentration of uranyl was detected by a UV–Vis absorption method at 650 nm (Arsenazo III).

The morphology of g-C3N4 and CNCD-2 were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the g-C3N4 has a typical layered porous structure. The microstructure of g-C3N4 was well maintained after CDs modification (Fig. 1b), and some CDs nanoparticles with a diameter of about 1 nm were observed on the surface of CNCD-2 (Fig. 1c) [42]. Next, the TEM elemental mapping of CNCD-2-U (CNCD-2 after photoreduction of uranium) was shown in Fig. 1e-1i. The main elemental constituent of CNCD-2-U was C, N, O and U, indicating that the CNCD-2 was successfully synthesized and the uranium was deposited on CNCD-2 after photoreduction.

The FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4 and CNCD were shown in Fig. 2 a. It can be seen that the characteristic peaks from 1150 to 1750 cm−1 and at 809 cm−1 of g-C3N4 are attributed to the typical aromatic C-N group and triazine ring, respectively, indicating that the g-C3N4 was successfully synthesized [32]. The characteristic peaks of CNCD were similar to those of g-C3N4, meaning that the introduced CDs have a negligible effect on the chemical structure of g-C3N4. There was no characteristic peak of CDs on the FT-IR spectrum of CNCD may be due to the low content. The element compositions of g-C3N4, CDs and CNCD were measured by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2b, the g-C3N4 consists mainly of N and C elements and the CDs consist mainly of C and O elements. For CNCD, there are mainly N, C elements and trace amount of O element. The oxygen contents (atomic %) of CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 are 1.04 %, 1.52 % and 1.92 %, respectively, which increase with the increase of quantum dots, further confirming that the CNCD was successfully obtained. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and CNCD-2 were shown in Fig. 2c-2f. The characteristic peaks of C 1 s at 284.92 and 288.26 eV were attributed to C-C and C-N = C, respectively. And the characteristic N 1 s peaks at 398.58 eV, 399.92 eV and 401.15 eV were attributed to C-N = C, N–(C)3 and N-H, respectively. Due to the low content of CDs, the peak position of N 1 s and C 1 s did not obviously shift after the introduction of the CDs, implying that the CDs had negligible effect on the structure of g-C3N4.

The phase structures of g-C3N4 and CNCD were measured by XRD patterns and shown in Fig. 3 a. The XRD pattern of g-C3N4 exhibited a strong characteristic peak at 27.4°, which attributed to the (002) crystal facet [41]. The diffraction peaks of CNCD were almost the same as those of g-C3N4, which was consistent with the results of FT-IR and XPS analysis. The BET surface areas and pore volumes of g-C3N4 and CNCD were measured by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. 3b, the specific surface areas of g-C3N4, CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 were 18.76, 28.54, 59.98 and 19.82 m2/g, respectively. In addition, the pore volumes of g-C3N4, CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 were 0.085, 0.122, 0.219 and 0.072 cm3/g, respectively. The CNCD-2 has the largest pore volume and specific surface area, which can offer plenty active sites for photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). Moreover, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of CNCD exhibit the type IV isotherms, which means that the CNCD was a mesoporous material [43]. To verify this, the pore width distributions of CNCD were also analyzed. It can be seen that the pore widths of CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 were 17.12 nm, 14.66 nm and 14.50 nm, respectively, which further indicates that the CNCD was a mesoporous material. The pore widths of CNCD were decreased with the increase of CDs, which indicates that the CDs were successfully loaded on porous g-C3N4.

The photocatalysis performance of as-synthesized materials for U(VI) was investigated under visible LED light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the removal rate of U(VI) was in the order CNCD-2 (95.26 %) > CNCD-1 (90.18 %) > CNCD-3 (90.02 %) > g-C3N4 (85.64 %). This order was the same as the that of BET surface area of these materials, indicating that the relatively large BET surface area can offer plenty active sites for photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). For comparison, the adsorption capacity of g-C3N4 and CNCD for U(VI) was also tested in dark (Fig. 4b). Obviously, the adsorption performance of these materials for U(VI) was much lower than that of photocatalytic reduction. Then, the CNCD-2 was used as a representative for subsequent studies because it has the best performance for photoreduction of U(VI).

It is well known that the pH value has a significant effect on the species distribution of U(VI) and the surface charges of materials. Thus, the photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD-2 was explored at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 4c, the removal rate of U(VI) by CNCD-2 was rapidly increased within the pH range from 3 to 5, and then balanced at pH 5–6. The removal rate was significantly lower at pH 3 and 4 than at pH 5 and 6. The Zeta potential (Fig. 4d) and U(VI) species (Fig. 4e) at various pH values were measured and calculated (Visual MINTEQ) [44], respectively. For acidic conditions (pH = 3 and 4), the U(VI) mainly exists as UO2 2+ ions and there is a high concentration of H+ in aqueous solution. Meanwhile, the surface of photocatalyst has a positive charge. Thus, either the competition between H+ and UO2 2+ or the repulsion between photocatalyst and UO2 2+ will lead to a lower removal rate for U(VI). When pH value was higher than 5, the surface positive charges of both photocatalyst and dominant uranium species were gradually decreased. Therefore, the competition between the H+ and UO2 2+ will decrease and the repulsion between the material and UO2 2+ will also decrease or even become attracted to each other, which leads to a higher removal rate of U(VI). The photoreduction of U(VI) by CNCD-2 under different initial U(VI) concentration was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4f, the removal rate of U(VI) by CNCD-2 could reach more than 95 % in the concentration range from 5 mg/L to 100 mg/L. This indicates that both the low and high concentrations of uranium can almost completely be removed by photocatalytic reduction.

To determine the selectivity removal of uranium by CNCD-2, the effect of interfering ions including Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+ and K+ on photoreduction of U(VI) was studied. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the removal rate of U(VI) could reach 91.25 % in the presence of interfering ions, meaning the CNCD-2 could selectively remove U(VI). To evaluate the economic and practical performance, the recyclability of CNCD-2 was investigated. After photocatalytic reduction, the CNCD-2-U was eluted in 0.1 M hydrochloric acid solution for 12 h, and then separated by centrifugation and washed with ultra-pure water three times before dried in vacuum. The dried material was used repeatedly for the next round of photoreduction of U(VI). As shown in Fig. 5b, the uranium removal rates of 5 cycle tests were 95.26 %, 94.12 %, 93.25 %, 92.15 % and 90.08 %, indicating that the reused CNCD-2 still maintained high photocatalytic activity for U(VI). The stability of CNCD-2 was characterized by FT-IR and XRD. As shown in Fig. 5c, except for the characteristic absorption peak of uranyl, the FT-IR spectrum of CNCD-2 has not been significantly changed after photocatalytic reduction or desorption. In addition, the XRD patterns of CNCD-2 and U-desorbed CNCD-2 were characterized and shown in Fig. 5d. Similar to the FT-IR spectrum, the XRD pattern of U-desorbed CNCD-2 also has no significant change compared with the original CNCD-2. The characterization results of FT-IR and XRD indicated that the CNCD-2 has sufficient stability during the cycle tests.

To clarify the mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of U(VI), XPS spectra of CNCD-2 and CNCD-2-U were obtained. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the characteristic double peaks of uranium appeared in the XPS spectrum of CNCD-2 after photocatalytic reduction. Then, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of U 4f for CNCD-2-U was characterized and shown in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that the U 4f5/2 peak can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 391.89 eV and 391.22 eV, which were attributed to the U(VI) and U(IV), respectively. Similarly, the U 4f7/2 peak can also be deconvoluted into two peaks at 381.48 eV and 380.60 eV, which were attributed to the U(VI) and U(IV), respectively [13]. These results confirmed that the CNCD-2 can reduce U(VI) to U(IV) under light. To further confirm the product of photocatalytic reduction, XRD patterns of CNCD-2 and CNCD-2-U were obtained and shown in Fig. 6c. Obviously, the (UO2)O2·2H2O (metastudtite, PDF#35–0571) was produced after photoreduction of U(VI). In other words, the rod-like uranium minerals in the TEM image of CNCD-2-U (Fig. 1d) were (UO2)O2·2H2O, implying that the U(VI) was transformed and immobilized to (UO2)O2·2H2O under visible LED light irradiation. Besides, there was no obvious diffraction peak of U(IV) product in the XRD pattern of CNCD-2-U, which may be due to its amorphous structure [45]. The photoluminescence (PL) can be used to characterize the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity of g-C3N4 was decreased after the introduction of CDs, meaning that the recombination of e−-h+ pairs was inhibited for CNCD [46]. Fig. 6e shows the transient photocurrent responses of g-C3N4 and CNCD. Compared to g-C3N4, CNCD has stronger, and CNCD-2 has the strongest photocurrent intensities. This indicates that CNCD-2 has strong visible light absorption capacity and excellent separation capacity of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs [47], which is consistent with the PL analysis. EIS Nyquist plots of g-C3N4 and CNCD were shown in Fig. 6f. It can be seen that the EIS Nyquist plot of CNCD-2 has a smaller semicircle than g-C3N4, CNCD-1 and CNCD-3, further confirming the faster interfacial charge transfer [48].

The UV–Vis DRS spectra of g-C3N4 and CNCD were obtained and shown in Fig. 7 a. Based on this, the Kubelka-Munk plots of g-C3N4 and CNCD were calculated by Kubelka-Munk equation [49]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the band gaps (E g) of g-C3N4, CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 were measured to be 2.54, 2.22, 2.17 and 2.32 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, according to the XPS VB spectra (Fig. 7c-7f) [50–51], the valence band potentials (E VB) of g-C3N4, CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 could be measured to be 2.24, 2.25, 2.23 and 2.30 eV, respectively.

Therefore, the conduction band potentials (E CB) of g-C3N4, CNCD-1, CNCD-2 and CNCD-3 can be obtained to be −0.30, 0.03, 0.06 and −0.02 eV, respectively, by the formula of ECB = EVB − Eg (Fig. 8 a). Obviously, the E CB of CNCD was lower than the reduction potentials of UO2 2+/UO2 (0.411 V) and UO2 2+/U4+ (0.267 V), implying that the photocatalytic reduction of uranyl by CNCD is theoretically feasible [16]. Based on the above analysis, the satisfactory photocatalysis activity of CNCD can be attributed to the effective separation of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs, strong visible light absorption capacity and narrow bandgap. In order to investigate the role of active species in the photoreduction of uranium by CNCD-2, the free radical trap experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8b, the trapping agents including CH3OH, KBrO3, tert-butanol (TBA) and para-benzoquinone (p-BQ), were used for trapping h+, e−, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), respectively. It can be seen that the removal capacity of CNCD-2 for U(VI) with methanol is significantly improved, indicating that the hole capture can greatly improve the photocatalytic efficiency. The removal rate of U(VI) was significantly reduced in the presence of KBrO3 or p-BQ, indicating that the e− and •O2 − play an important role in the photocatalysis process. However, the role of •OH is negligible since the TBA has no obvious effect on the photoreduction of U(VI). The species of free radicals in the photocatalysis process was determined by EPR. As shown in Fig. 8c and 8d, the characteristic quadruple peaks of •OH and •O2 − appeared in the light while no signal was appeared in dark. This suggests that both •O2 − and •OH were indeed generated in the process of photoreduction of U(VI).

Thus, based on the results of the above mechanism study, the process of photocatalytic reduction of uranium by CNCD was summarized in Equations (1)–(6) and Fig. 9 . The CNCD was excited by visible light irradiation to produce e− and h+ (Equation (1)). The e− can react with O2 to form •O2 − (Equation (2)), and the resulting •O2 − and e− could convert the soluble UO2 2+ to (UO2)O2·2H2O (Equations (3) and (4)). The h+ can react with H2O to form •OH (Equation (5)), and the resulting •OH and h+ could oxidize methanol to CO2 and H2O (Equation (6)) [52].
(1) g-C3N4/CDs + hv → g-C3N4/CDs (e- + h+)
(2) O2 + e- → •O2 –
(3) UO2 2+ + 2e- → UO2
(4) UO2 + 4H+ + 2•O2 – → (UO2)O2·2H2O
(5) H2O + h+ → •OH + H+
(6) CH3OH + 6 h+ + 6•OH → CO2 + 5H2O

In summary, carbon dots modified porous g-C3N4 (CNCD) photocatalysts were synthesized by thermal condensation and used for the photoreduction of U(VI) under visible LED light irradiation. The as-synthesized CNCD-2 has high photocatalytic activity over a wide range of U(VI) concentrations. Moreover, the CNCD-2 showed excellent selectivity and recyclability for photocatalytic uranium removal. Further mechanism studies revealed that the introduction of carbon dots could increase visible light absorption, inhibit photoinduced electron-hole pairs recombination and facilitate interfacial charge separation of porous g-C3N4. The soluble U(VI) was transformed and immobilized to (UO2)O2·2H2O on the surface of CNCD-2. These results indicate that the CNCD is a promising photocatalyst for the remediation of uranium-containing wastewater.

Zhongran Dai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shang Zhao: Investigation. Junjie Lian: Investigation. Le Li: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Dexin Ding: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Excellent Young Scholars (2020JJ3028); the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (21A0259); and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51704170, 12175103). Additionally, we thank Yuan Gao (China University of Mining and Technology) for his helpful discussion.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanism of uranium release from uranium mill tailings under long-term exposure to simulated acid rain: Geochemical evidence and environmental implication
Environ. Pollut.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yin, J. Sun, Y. Chen, J. Wang, J. Shang, N. Belshaw, C. Shen, J. Liu, H. Li, W. Linghu, T. Xiao, X. Dong, G. Song, E. Xiao, D. Chen, Mechanism of uranium release from uranium mill tailings under long-term exposure to simulated acid rain: Geochemical evidence and environmental implication, Environ. Pollut. 244 (2019) 174-181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photoreduction of U(VI) on C3N4 by Cr(VI) and bisphenol A: ESR, XPS and EXAFS investigation
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.H. Wang, H. Guo, N. Zhang, Z.S. Chen, B.W. Hu, X.K. Wang, Enhanced photoreduction of U(VI) on C3N4 by Cr(VI) and bisphenol A: ESR, XPS and EXAFS investigation, Environ. Sci. Technol. 53 (2019) 6454-6461.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Redox-switchable carboranes for uranium capture and release
Nature
